comments.content:
What a coincidence! I've been working through Lawlor's book for the past couple of weeks now. The book is quite approachable for a beginner in sacred geometry, and the insights are both profound and stunningly obvious once they're explained to you, to the point where you wonder how you could have missed it. Mathematical education being what it is these days (Paul Lockhart has covered that ground pretty extensively in his Mathematician's Lament), it's amazing that most students subjected to it escape with any appreciation for the subject at all.

A couple of more personal questions this week:

1. Where do you get your news from? I’ve been looking for a while for an outlet that avoids the pitfalls of current discourse and hasn’t gone overboard with clickbait and sensationalism. There doesn't seem to be much middle ground in the media ecosystem between corporate propaganda, paranoid doom-mongering and vacuous clickbait.

2. Did the career of a writer differ in any ways from your initial expectations? What were the most significant obstacles on the business side of things? Did you have any trouble with the human element, such as relationships with contributors, editors, publishers, etc.?

3. Is it possible to reach a balance between doing what you love but can’t make money from, and what you have to do to earn a living? Unfortunately, many people can’t combine what they love with what pays the bills (myself included). This leaves you with the option of doing what you love in your spare time, and spending most of your waking time on something you would rather not be doing. The problem with that, obviously enough, is that many jobs are so draining that you barely have the energy to do anything else. How did you manage before you were a full-time author?

4. What are you reading right now? What problems are currently on your mind?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) I read exclusively foreign news sources these days -- the US media is a complete waste of time. I like to balance competing viewpoints against each other: for example, BBC vs. RT -- they almost always take opposite sides on any question of importance. World news also takes on a very different shape if you see how it looks from India, Japan, East Africa, or what have you.

2) By the time I actually started my writing career I had a good idea what I was in for -- I'd read quite a few books on the business end of writing. The major problem I faced was figuring out how to find suitable publishers on the fringes who were interested in the kind of weird things I like to write. As for the human element, well, of course -- as someone with Aspergers syndrome I'm not good at that.

3) My approach was to work in low-paying, grueling, miserable jobs, so that I had an extra incentive to get published!

4) Right now I'm rereading John Crowley's novel Little, Big and Matila Ghyka's The Golden Number, a book on sacred geometry. The problems on my mind? Fine details of the plot of a novel I've recently started, my first post-tentacle novel; certain elements of another book I'm working on, a manual of occult training; and the upcoming Cancer ingress and its political and economic implications.

replies.title: (no subject)

1) According to the occult philosophy I'm familiar with, it's a one-to-one relationship all the way down.

2) How could you possibly tell if a being was actually omnipotent and omniscient? Nobody's yet come up with an omnipotentiometer, or what have you. All you can say for sure is that a given god appears to be very, very powerful. Since the miracles attributed to YHVH in times recent enough for good evidence to survive are no more remarkable than those attributed to many other deities, yes, my guess is that his worshipers got caught up in what Alfred North Whitehead called "metaphysical flattery" and attributed to their god qualities that no being has, or can have.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: 2) Somewhat related, but as a polytheist, are you committed to the ultimate Unity of the cosmos, in the sense of a (Neo)Platonic One, where apparent multiplicities and dualities are resolved? Or is it Many all the way down? If it's the former, wouldn't this One be pretty close to what monotheists call God, although it wouldn't necessarily be a personal god?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
That's a common misunderstanding of Neoplatonism. The One is not the ultimate unity of the cosmos; the One is the principle by which the cosmos exists. It is apart from everything else, and has no qualities or characteristics, not even existence if this is understood as being something distinct from nonexistence. It is not a god. It does not think, act, experience, or care, much less pick one specific Middle Eastern ethnic group as its Chosen People or have a son who it sends to earth to die on a cross to redeem humanity from its sins.

The cosmos is a unity in a certain sense, in that it participates in the One, but it's a unity that embraces infinite diversity, and the multiplicities and dualities are not resolved in it -- they play freely from everlasting to everlasting. So, yes, I accept Neoplatonism as a very useful set of metaphors for the nature of reality -- knowing that metaphors are as close as our brains can get to that subject -- and no, I don't make the howling logical error of confusing a ground of being with a divine person.

replies.title: The YHWH jealous god thing: a datapoint (ymmv)
replies.content:
Hello

I’d like to chime in on the question about working with the judeo-christian God, if I may.

I’m a former lukewarm Baptist, then 20+ years devout atheist, then practicing polytheist (yes, thanks to you JMG - your World Full of Gods is a most excellent book) and now once again a practicing Christian. I was the one who wrote to you last summer, about feeling like I was being called to go to a certain Episcopal church. I’ve seen the question about working with YHWH or Jesus come up a few times, so I thought I’d share my experience.

While I was still a polytheist, I was aware of YHWH’s existence, but pretty much ignored him. He didn’t seem to mind. (Somebody, maybe it was on this blog, said He requires worship of his chosen people (the jews) and accepts but does not require worship from others. That seems to jibe with the feeling I got). If a non-christian calls on him, he may or may not help, like any other deity who may or may not respond to your requests.

I started feeling called, however, and went to church. Skipped out communion the first time, as a voice told me “Don’t do this unless you are willing to commit”. The next Sunday, I committed. Was officially confirmed in the church in February, but that felt like a rubber stamp: the real confirmation was done by God months before.

So, for you non-christians who are wondering, I would say, just be polite. He doesn’t owe you anything, but as JMG pointed out, often answers non-christian calls for help. If you have dedicated yourself to him, however, you had better not call on any other gods. I made the mistake of doing the CGD version of things ONE time after my conversion, and proceeded to have a very.bad.day. I now do a SoP calling on the various Names of Jesus and ending with a binding prayer (think of a knight offering his sword to his king) and things have been wonderful. I guess I’m still a polytheist in the sense that I believe there are other gods (and no, they are not the same thing as demons) but I am not to worship them. I may greet them politely when encountered, and I still greet the nature spirits in my garden and the little cluster of (what I think are) air elementals that play in my oak tree, but think of it like a married man politely greeting a woman who is not his wife. There are definitely limits!

Anyhow, that’s how I see it. To paraphrase Richard Bach, “Everything in this comment may be wrong”

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
2) If the God (or the highest impersonal principle - whatever it is called) were omniscient, then from its point of view, the existence of the universe would be a pointless experiment whose outcome is already pre-determined. If such a being has all the attributes of the Absolute, then why would it need to do such an experiment whose outcome is already known to it?
Also, the idea of omniscience and its direct implication (pre-determination) are not compatible with occult philosophy. Because i) Occult philosophy assumes that all of the beings in the universe have some degree of free will, depending on their degree of development (from the simplest particle to the most complex life form). ii) If the Cosmos manifests itself as a Universal Mind and the events are ideas in this Mind, then the exact knowledge of an event and its occurrence must be simultaneous.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
You're looking at the problem with the logic of existence within the parameters of time and space, where change always entails the passage of time, for example from an earlier cause to a later effect. In the part of our existence that lies wholly outside of time and space, there seems to be a thing somewhat analogus to "change," but without any temporal component, any "before" and "after."

(I'm sorry if this is unclear. It's probably not really possible to understand these words of mine without at least some dim memory, some past perception, of that mode of our existence, where everything that "was" and "is" and "will be" is co-present, no matter "where" it might be.)

Anyway, in this mode of existence the logic of ordinary existence doesn't work, and two logically contradictory statements about "things" in it can both be equally accurate ("true") within the limits of human language. In that mode of existence, it is not paradoxical, but highly instructive, to assert, for example, that "the Gods exist" and "the Gods do not exist" are both equally "true." What the Gods "do" that cannot be better expressed by any other word than our verb "to exist," is so poorly captured even by that word, and by the rules of logic our poor brains are trapped within, that both the affirmation and the denial are equally accurate and equally inaccurate.

I hope this helps clear things up just a little ...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Such talismans currently exist -- I've seen several -- and we have ample knowledge about how such things are made and consecrated.

2) Normally it's not a big deal. Egypt, like some other ancient societies, had an archaic technology that allowed some specially prepared souls to step out of the cycle of reincarnation; that was later abused by people who saw it as a way to cheat death, and eventually the techniques were lost and it simply became a burial ceremony gone through by rote, with no effect.

3) Such people aren't hypothetical -- they make up the majority of our species. People don't begin to contact their Higher Selves, even subconsciously, until they've finished learning the lessons of the human level and are getting ready to move on. They have memory, will, and imagination, but only in reflected forms, so the memory is fragmentary and unstable as Hod, the will is mostly submerged in the passions of Netzach, and the imagination simply rehashes the existing stock of images in Yesod, the Treasure House of Images.

4) Aleister Crowley didn't reach the pinnacle of magic. Come on -- he started out life rich, intelligent, talented, handsome, and charismatic, and ended up as a burnt-out drug addict in a small town nursing home with an estate worth 16 shillings and a name that he had personally made a laughing stock on three continents. If that's the result of a life devoted to magic, you're doing it wrong. Crowley crashed and burned on the rocks of his own overdeveloped ego; it's a common occupational hazard of those whose magical practice isn't guided by a good clear sense of magical ethics.

Hi JMG,

I have question about exchanging energies with trees. Yesterday I tried to do it for the first time. I found a nice pine at the edge of a forrest, went to it, touched it with my hand and asked, if it was ok.
I did not feel any negative Response, but I should mention, that I am a muggle and usually feel next to nothing.
I proceeded with the technique from the Druidry handbook, I took a sitting position with my back to the tree and relaxed. I imagined the tree and its life force.
Since the atmosphere was clear, peaceful and refreshing and I had a bit of an developing headache, I went on and tried to „clear it“ I concentrated on the headache and how it went away.
12 minutes passed and I went back inside and after an hour and a half developed a splitting headache and exhaustion. Thinking I did something wrong I even consulted my daily divination, which did predict, that I will rush ahead into trouble. Great. I asked if I did something wrong and got MOR-, PHAGOS-, STRAIF+
Something unexpected happened, because you did not head the wisdom, results of ones own actions.

- did I impose on the tree and got a retaliation?
- or did I ask for this myself, since my intentionally was focused on the headache in the negative?
- or was the exhaustion a consequence of my inexperience?
- I sleept it off and am feeling better. What did I do wrong and how can I cure it?
- did I in any way harm the tree and how can I make it good?

Best regards,
V

replies.title: Sometimes a headache is just a headache
replies.content: The following is only my opinion: I think the tree wanted to exchange energy with you, especially since your headache was temporarily relieved. Since a tree can't cure you of all your ills instantaneously, the headache came back because it is warm outside and everyone's sinuses are on the warpath. Mine too -- this time of year is gorgeous but beauty comes with a price! Trees are wonderful creatures but they're nowhere near omnipotent when it comes to human headaches; let's say you had a headache because you were constipated. The tree can take some of your constipated energy to help things start moving but eating right and getting on the toilet is one hundred percent up to you. You also drew Oghams that I interpret as positive. In my estimation, the Forfedha, just like Tarot trumps, are too complex to interpret as ill-dignified and I only interpret them that way when I ask a blunt yes/no question and draw a single Forfedha. I think Mor means "sea change" (http://druidogham.wordpress.com) and it is indicating a great shift in your life. Being on the Path now would be a major shift... just saying. Phagos is the influence of Hermes, the power of writing things down, mastery of some aspect of the mental plane. Straif, which I do read as well-dignified as it is not a Forfedha, is necessary challenge: what doesn't kill you makes you stronger. You are being proofed.

comments.title: Strange memory
comments.content:
Dear JMG,
A few years ago, I was talking with some friends and started spouting strange things about Robert E. Lee. I am Canadian and have never studied your Civil War or much of your history at all.

I did not understand why I was saying what I was saying but the words tumbled out of my mouth with a will of their own. I said Lee was a great man, a superior general, a paladin and I wanted to say he was against slavery but that seemed too strange. Later, I looked up a couple of biographies of General Lee and he was everything I had said about him and he was indeed against slavery.

Some time later, while doing Kundalini yoga, the music in the studio changed to a sad violin solo and I had a vision of a pair of shaggy black horses in the winter hauling a wagon with an empty box. Neither the horses nor the wagon had any insignia on them but I was pretty sure I was seeing a scene from the American Civil War and I was overcome with sadness, guilt and self-loathing. I think the wagon had either unloaded bodies or coffins. I wept and I can go for years without shedding tears. Later, I recalled the vision was from my left eye only.

I stopped doing Kundalini yoga as I think it was bringing things up before their time. My question for you is, do you think this was someone showing me things from their past or was I remembering things from my past? I have tried to do divinations on this but have received odd, inconclusive answers. One witch friend said I was haunting myself, that I was the soldier and another incarnation at the same time.

I believe this could be an ancestor as one of my Grandmothers came from the States as a child a very long time ago. He, or another man in a cape, (I think people might have been seeing the cape-shouldered great coats favoured in that war) has been seen here by a couple of friends and he or some other male disembodied person greeted an empath friend while I was helping her on with her coat. My daughter could smell a man, very strong, rank smell, in her room one night. I apologized on his behalf, explaining he had a very rough war.

These things have all happened over a number of years. Sometimes, ages go past here without a single oddball thing happening.

Please let me know what you think.
Maxine

replies.title: Re: Strange memory
replies.content: It sounds to me like a past life memory surfacing.

replies.title: Re: Strange memory
replies.content:
Thanks for that. If that was my last lifetime, I must have resisted reincarnation vigorously. I am finally starting to really enjoy being alive and hope to achieve a good result in this life to be a more kind and cheerful person next time around. My family and friends agree I had been a man and a very bad one. I will try to do better this time.
Thanks so much for the magic in my life. You have been a great help.
Maxine

comments.title: Lost in the Woods, Trying to Find the Path Again
comments.content:
Salutations JMG! If the subject is sacred geometry, might I recommend the Constructing the Universe series for those who like workbooks? Here's some quick questions and then a larger question I'm wrestling with:

1) I've heard some people say the Donald Tyson edition of Agrippa's Three Books of Occult Philosophy has been modified according to Tyson's own tastes. At the very least, a Youtube review channel shows that the printing is horrendous (some pictures - inserted for flavour and not part of the original text - are so degraded they consist mostly of flat black ink, as if they're the product of 20 photocopies of photocopies). Joseph Peterson is a very respected scholar, but I can't seem to find a printed version of his translation of the Three Books. What version of the Three Books is sitting on your shelf?

2) In Geomancy, when trying to choose a new form of magical practice, is that an 8th or 9th house issue? The 9th is "occult philosophy and divination" but what if you're doing more than just studying out of books such as performing daily rituals? Wouldn't that classify it as "magic performed by/for the Querent" as part of the 8th house? This leads into the big question...

3-Big Question) I'm feeling unsure as to what magical path to choose. I've been sporadically working Bruce Frantzis' Water Style Qi Gong in addition to Geomancy and trying to meditate consistently, but I'm starting to think that maybe I should first ground myself in a more complete tradition first before going on to experimental magic. I see a great deal of worth in Taoism, both in its philosophy and practices that seem way more powerful and unique than anything I've seen. But it's difficult to break into as a westerner, and there is no self regulating system with checks and balances like Golden Dawn based systems. My plan was to develop my energy body with Qi Gong (my energy body seems about as responsive as clay) and move from there to a system of Astral Projection and Energy Work that is also fairly Qi Gong inspired, then once mastered move to Franz Bardon's system. Note that I'm not jumping around from system to system, but I've previously thought that each system is a prerequisite to waking up my sluggish energy body and giving me the tools to work the next system even more effectively. Like I need to learn metalworking to forge a shovel, and I need a shovel to begin gardening, and I need to begin gardening to grow herbs, and I need to grow herbs to make a fine tea.

I'd love to experiment with the way Taoist practices interact with Western Energy work, but should I try getting established in a more accessible tradition first (even if it doesn't inspire me)? Or is this a test that I should meet by knuckling down and continuing with my Qi Gong practices?

My goal with magic is to become psychic/clairvoyant enough that I can experiment and observe directly the energies of the human body and living things. So I guess I'm looking for a tradition that is fairly etheric focused like Qi Gong.

Sorry for the shotgun spread of text, I thought if you understood where I'm coming from you might know how best to point me. Here's some bullet point questions:
3a) Is staying the course advisable, or would I benefit more efficiently from a tradition that is more complete than Taoism and designed to be learned by westerners in solitude from a book?
3b) Does the Celtic Golden Dawn work with the etheric, etheric/astral projection and developing clairvoyance? It seems to me the Golden Dawn traditions are very Astral/Mental plane based.
3c) Will working with the Qi Gong circuits lock me out from practicing western styles of magic (and vice versa)?

replies.title: Re: Lost in the Woods, Trying to Find the Path Again
replies.content:
1) I used Tyson when that was what was available. These days, when I want to consult Agrippa, I check a PDF of the Latin text. I know that may not be too helpful to you!

2) Magical practice is 8th, magical theory is 9th. If you're looking at taking up practice, that's 8th.

3a) That's something only you can decide.

3b) No, its techniques are standard Golden Dawn-derived, thus primarily astral and mental. There's a significant etheric component via alchemy, but not the sort of thing you're working with.

3c) Very probably yes.

replies.title: Re: Lost in the Woods, Trying to Find the Path Again
replies.content:
1) Hmm. Dr. Peterson has a pdf up on his wonderful site, but I find I learn better from physical paper. Bookbinding is one of the traditional skills I've wanted to learn for a while, perhaps once the local leather vendor opens back up I'll have to pay them a visit.

3b) Do you know of any traditions other than Taoism that are focused on the etheric (aside from maybe natural magic)? Mesmerism perhaps?

3c) Well darn. I'd really like to help obscure Taoist concepts enrich western magic, I think there's a lot to offer, it's just obscured by the lack of translated texts and the dozens of different methods with the same name. For example, the "spiralling dragon currents" of the Feng Shui traditions that focus more on the earth than on human buildings sound like a match for the Telluric currents you describe in The Secret of the Temple. The "Creation of the Golden Foetus" (beginning of creating a sort of perfected immortal spirit body) also sounds similar in its goal to the perfection of your Mental Sheath, or the attainment of Gwynfdd, except it begins in the lowest Dan Tien rather than out there in the Astral/Mental somewhere. I just wish I had more guidance, I think I know how those first Golden Dawn adepts felt trying to piece together things from bad translations and limited sources, except I'm no adept.

4) You've said in the past that your Druid Magic courses are similar to Taoism. Did you mean only philosophically, or do they share some mechanics? The downward sun current and the upward earth current in the DMH sound very much like the downward Water Qi and the Upward Fire Qi, except the downward chi is pulled in through the crown of the head (but NOT the brain) through the face, over the tongue, down the throat and into the body. The moon current also sounds similar to "The Fusion of Fire and Water" practice, which combines both Qi currents. The Taoist "stillness meditation" neccessary for Frantzis' Qi Gong work is also identical to the "receptive meditation" in the Druidry Handbook.

5) Why are these systems necessarily incompatible? I know it's because they treat energy in the body differently, but what specific differences cause the mix up?
Is it the Qi Gong exercises which concentrate energy in specific spots, whereas western magic doesn't?

6) I know you've said mixing Qi Gong with your own practice messed up your kidneys for a while. What exact practices were you doing, and what sources were you learning from? I've read and reread the following thread, but some more data points might help:
https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/80698.html

replies.title: Re: Lost in the Woods, Trying to Find the Path Again
replies.content:
3b) Central European occultism of the sort exemplified by Franz Bardon. There are many other authors in the same field, but next to none of them have been translated into English. If you're up for learning German, you might be able to to very well.

3c) Understood. Keep in mind that you're playing with fire, though.

4) The similarities are mostly philosophical, as far as I know.

5) That's a good question that can only be settled via experimentation. I'm not even sure that they're necessarily incompatible -- just that I've heard from a number of people who've tried to mix them and who had health problems as a result.

6) I studied one of the old Yang styles of taijiquan from Sifu Andrew Dale in Seattle and then from his student Sifu Gene Burnett in Ashland OR. That system included several qigong sets, including Emei Mountai qigong and Tian Shan qigong. That interacted with the magical work I was doing to give me a serious yang kidney qi depletion, which took years to cure.

replies.title: Re: Lost in the Woods, Trying to Find the Path Again
replies.content:
3b) Could you list as many of those authors as you can think of? Learning German might be in the cards someday, it's one of the languages I'd like to learn (the first is Latin, which I've resolved to begin learning by June if not sooner).

Do you happen to know if anybody has tried combining Bardon with Qi Gong exercises? That would be an interesting experiment. His "accumulation of the elements" seemed exactly like an etheric exercise when I read it. Is there anything in Bardon that comes to mind as being incompatible with Qi work?

6) Alright, I'll look into those sets and how they work, although any style with Yang in the title automatically sets off warning bells in my head. Mantak Chia is one master with particularly Yang style methods and quite a few people report getting burned with his methods (no pun intended).

If you feel like reading about Qi Gong, the system I'm practicing is by Bruce Frantzis, and can be best explained in "Opening the Energy Gates of Your Body" and "The Great Stillness." Bruce Frantzis was one of the first westerners who journeyed into China when they opened their borders, and he spent a lot of time in China and Japan seeking ever more advanced martial arts practices (he's a very adept martial artist in multiple styles). During his time in Japan he zeroed in on the fact that some sort of ki energy was at work behind some of the most powerful practices, and these practices traced their lineage to Taoists and Chan Buddhists from China. He then went seeking masters in China and happened upon a very old master who had studied as a boy at one of the last monasteries that taught what he calls "Water Method." The Water style was apparently in the process of dissolving (heh) and dying out in favour of newer Yang and Buddist-hybrid forms of Qi Gong. The tradition officially passed on to Frantzis claimed to trace it's lineage directly back to Lao Tse, although I'm not sure if I believe that part. Either way, Frantzis has come across some very obscure, untranslated forms of Qi Gong. In one of his books he mentions a kind of White Crane style Qi Gong that involved "vibrating" his Qi at tremendous speeds, and said that it made him strong enough to shatter bones with a single strike during martial arts, but he abandoned it when he realized it was making him "subtly psychotic" and induced subtle levels of megalomania and indifference to the pain caused in others in its practitioners. Fascinating and terrifying stuff!

Frantzis recommends Tai Chi but says Ba Gua and Hsing-I are much more powerful and complete, so for anybody experimenting with eastern movement practices, there's a couple more inquiries for your experimentation.

replies.title: Re: Lost in the Woods, Trying to Find the Path Again
replies.content:
3b) Georg Lomer, Anton Joseph Kirchweger, and Franz Anton Mesmer are the ones who come to mind.

6) You do know that the Yang in the Yang style is not the same character as Yang in Yin-Yang, I trust...

As for Kumar Frantzis, yes, I'm familiar with his work. He had quite a colorful reputation back when I was plugged into the tai chi scene.

replies.title: Re: Lost in the Woods, Trying to Find the Path Again
replies.content:
Basically, Tyson just reprinted the 1651 printed English translation (the only one ever made) of Agrippa's Latin, modernizing the spelling and adding paragraph breaks for ease of reading. (He also added a huge number of notes and appendices.) The 1651 translation is a usable one, but not a really great one. It would be possible to make a better one ("had we but world enough and time"). You can download a PDF of the 1651 edition here:

https://onedrive.live.com/?authkey=%21AEsyXVTCFS9XIKk&cid=2709E41E745116A5&id=2709E41E745116A5%21238&parId=2709E41E745116A5%21231&o=OneUp

There is now a good critical edition of Agrippa's original Latin, edited by V. Perrone Compagni (Brill, 1992) but it costs multiple hundreds of dollars. You can download Agrippa's original first edition in Latin (1553) from a number of German and Swiss rare book libraries. A PDF of what seems to be C. G. Jung's own personal copy of the 1533 edition is available from the "Swiss Electronic Library" (www.e-lib.ch).

replies.title: Re: Lost in the Woods, Trying to Find the Path Again
replies.content:
I know I'm late for the party, but since someone can stumble upon your recommendation in the future, one always must check at the publisher first.

It is not uncommon to find second-hand books, specially on magic, at astronomical prices, You can buy Agrippa's book directly from Brill for €125.00 / $155.00 (I think this price does not include shipping).

Link: https://brill.com/view/title/1024

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
If you can handle Shingon, that's easy as pie, as I worked out a Shingon SoP years ago and found it very effective in practice. The Godai Nyorai or five Tathagatas plus Sakyamuni Buddha are assigned to the points as follows:

East: Fukūjōju Nyorai (Amoghasiddhi Buddha)
West: Amida Nyorai (Amitabha Buddha)
South: Hōshō Nyorai (Ratnasambhava Buddha)
North: Ashuku Nyorai (Aksobhya Buddha)
Below: Shaka Nyorai (Sakyamuni Buddha)
Above: Dainichi Nyorai (Vairocana Buddha)

(These are not their traditional directional attributions but they are the ones that match the elements.)

You'll have to judge whether this is compatible with your current Vajrayana practice, but iirc the five wisdom buddhas are pretty generally revered.

I got so frustrated about this last week and in the past that I felt like I wanted to rush with the DMH, that I wanted to go ahead to the Grail working to get 'fixed' and so I've clamped down on that desire in order to proceed at a natural pace.

If the tendency of the mind to fall into these sorts of ruts is something that magical practice is in part meant to help overcome, how can one avoid this habit? Just by being aware of it?

Thank you!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I have something of the same tendency--for me, it's likely part of an anxiety disorder (and possibly inattentive-version ADD)--and what I've found helpful is performing a version of the Fourfold Breath. The need to count and pay attention to my breathing on its own tends to disrupt whatever "hamster wheel" my thoughts have been on, and then it's easier to either find another subject or be present.

Of course, that requires me to notice what I'm doing and have the willpower to stop and do the breathing, which is another kettle of fish, but it's a help.

For wondering about different lives...two things come to mind. First, if these are changes you can make, you might want to sit down and make a list of realistic pros and cons--"If I move to Wisconsin, I'll be in some very pretty country, but I'll miss my friends here," for example--and then let that sit for a month or two before reading it. If the pros still outweigh the cons, hey, maybe start trying to do that thing!

If the things you're thinking about aren't possible, try and keep in mind that we almost always can imagine the good parts of those situations. We often don't think about how they might not turn out well, or not turn out much better than what we have now--and we can't really see all of the things coming up for us that we might miss if we *did* have X or Y. Again, I struggle with keeping that in mind at times, but when I can, it's useful.

(It makes it easier if you can think of a parallel case where you were really disappointed at the time but two or three years down the road saw that it was for the best: I was devastated when one of my HS boyfriends dumped me right before college, for example, but after sophomore year, I realized all the cool things I'd gotten to see and do that probably wouldn't have happened if he and I were still dating, for a number of reasons. And then I read over his letters to me *from* when we were dating and realized that, while the guy in question was perfectly nice, there was nothing there I wanted any more. When I can keep that in mind, it makes similar "but IF ONLY" situations easier to weather.)

comments.title: Barbarous words
comments.content: What are your thoughts on 'Barbarous words' used in some magical texts?  Are they words that have no connection to any language spoken in the past, or are they words that have simply been butchered in transcription over time?

replies.title: Re: Barbarous words
replies.content: It really varies from case to case. In ancient times, there were intricate sets of sound symbolism that were used to construct magical words of power -- you can find traces of the results in the Graeco-Egyptian magical papyri and in some of the Gnostic gospels. You also have words that are revealed by spirits, in which case anything can be involved. Finally, words do get garbled in transcription, but that doesn't make them useless -- there's an almost Darwinian process by which words mutate into more and more effective magical vocal sounds. Plenty of medieval names of power are complete gibberish, but if you use them as given you can get excellent results.

comments.title: Astrological ages
comments.content:
What is an astrological age? We have talked a lot about the change from the age of picses to the age of Aquarius. It is a very interesting topic but I don’t really understand what these ages are and also why are the dates involved not know exactly? Everything else in astrology seems pretty exact.

Thanks much

replies.title: Re: Astrological ages
replies.content:
Okay, let's start with the precession of the equinoxes. The points in the heavens where the sun is located at the moment of spring and fall equinoxes creep slowly backwards against the backdrop of the stars, at a rate of 1° every 72 years or so. That means that the stars that used to be behind the sun at the moment of spring equinox in 2000 BCE, let's say, are a long way from that position now.

The moment of the spring equinox is 0° Aries in terms of standard Western astrological practice, but the stars of Aries are nowhere near that place. That doesn't mean that the sign is wrong -- the signs of the zodiac are not the constellations that gave them their names back in Greek times, they're 30° wedges of the ecliptic counted from the positions of the equinoxes -- but the fact that the stars are shifting reveals long slow changes in the pattern of celestial energies on earth.

That's what makes astrological ages. When the position of the spring equinox slips into a portion of space associated with a different sign, everything changes. The great problem is that nobody knows where the boundaries are. The constellations are very rough markers at best, and there are no dotted lines in the sky showing where one ends and another begins. so various secondary methods have to be used. Eventually it'll all be worked out and we'll be able to tell exactly how long it is until the next age of the world begins, but that's still a long way off!

David BTL

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
To expand on the question slightly--

With four elements, each potentially outer and inner, one has a 4x4 (16 cell) matrix. As there are 16 figures, one might expect a natural symmetry to result in a 1-to-1 mapping from the the set of figures onto the cells this matrix (an invertible function, as it were). Alas, this is not so.

Specifically, we find four "double assignments," which result in four cells of the matrix going unfilled. Using the convention (outer,inner), one has:

Both Carcer and Caput Draconis at (earth,earth)
Both Populus and Via at (water, water)
Both Puer and Acquisito at (fire,air)
Both Albus and Puella at (air,water)

The unfilled cells are:

(water,earth)
(earth,water)
(air,air) as I mentioned originally, and
(fire,water)

The first two of the "missing" pairings at least possess a symmetry and the third (laying on the diagonal of the matrix) is by definition symmetrical with itself. However, the last "missing" pair is most definitely asymmetrical as Laetitia is assigned to its counterpart of (water,fire).

Switching from math-speak to layman's language, what gives?

--David BTL

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Here again, you're in territory that as far as I know nobody has yet explored. The inner elements I used came from one source, the outer elements from a different source. Their relationship in terms of the deeper structure of geomancy is to my knowledge unexplored ground.

The work you're doing is great, btw. Please keep at it!

It is as though I don't make any impression or remain in others' memories when not directly in their field of view. Like a wraith, not a fully real being.

It is true that my social skills are not so strong - I am not at ease with people generally - but neither are they in the abysmal category. There is a monotonous quality to the repetition of these patterns. Questions to medicos about Asperger's syndrome, which seems to share the affective qualities of my situation, keep getting dismissed as though, to them, I am obviously not that. It is not as though I need to learn one key thing: not getting any closer to feeling like an authentic person however much else I do learn has taught me that that hypothetical key thing probably is not attainable.

kallianeira.

comments.title: Dion Fortune Society, Nameless God etc.

Dear JMG

Several questions for this week - thank you in advance!

1. I saw someone post on the other blog about pastlife memories (or was it this blog?) anyway: it occurred to me: if you can clearly remember previous lives and what you did and how they ended - does that mean you can remember the languages you spoke then too? I am in England, plus I really liked Dion Fortune’s books, so I was wondering what you thought about me joining her organization.

They have two correspondence courses - one “supervised” (only available to UK residents) and one “unsupervised” (available to anyone anywhere but does not include some of the mind training exercises which need to be supervised).

Successfully passing the Supervised course entitles you to apply to join the actual Society and study further and participate in rituals etc. I believe this is a process that takes years - first to do the correspondence course and then join etc.

I know you think highly of Dion Fortune so I am assuming her Society still do good work. I guess what I am really asking is if there are any red flags for me to consider, and what it will add for me to join the Society (starting with the correspondence course) vs going it alone.

Also, as a newbie, am I better off doing a year or so of discursive meditation and SOP and then joining, or better off just joining now (maybe read a few more Fortune books first) and letting the Society take care of all that training? I have a general interest in the occult and particularly training my mind and experiencing the unseen world. I think I’d like to go more towards the practical rituals side and less in terms of the “knowledge” side - but honestly I’m so new, I’m not sure those preferences have really been tested.

(I am also into aikido quite seriously if that makes any difference - although no exposure to its esoteric side)

3. I have been praying for many years (last thing before bed, every single night) on a daily basis to an “unknown god”. As in, I have a Hindu background and originally started off praying to various Hindu gods, then kind of naturally evolved without planning to the “ultimate godhead” or “cosmic unity” kind of force, and then that kind of slowly changed again over the years to an individual god with no name - more like a force.

There are no words, but I get the “sense” that he is male. Every time I pray to him it’s slightly different - some times it feels like he’s very nearby. Sometimes I’ll get a sense of “amusement” or “busyness”. No real specifics or answers to questions.

I guess what I am asking is how do I know what is “real” and what is my brain making stuff up - what is just random thoughts coming into my brain that I interpret as his messages? These things just feel like random thoughts popping into my brain.

I know it is a question of practice and I will get better at it (after all I didn’t notice *anything* until a few months ago) but are there any rules of thumb to distinguish? Or characteristics of “real” communication? This is very far from “a voice in my head speaking clearly”.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Good afternoon, JMG

My question today is about imagination and visualization.

I have noticed in my efforts to improve this faculty that I experience what feel like two different kinds of visualizations/imagined objects or places.

One kind is when I am in a state of actively focused concentration on forming a sign or a landscape. It is a lot of work, and feels like the mental equivalent of tense muscles. I can do it with an acceptably good amount of visual detail, but it gets to be a strain after a while. These imaginings have a dense feel to them. It is difficult to perceive information about these places or objects in a receptive mode as I seem to be expending a lot of mindspace in creating them and maintaining their presence. If I let up on my concentration they lose their fullness.

The other kind is much more gossamer and relaxed. An example- I lightly but clearly direct my mind to say SEASHORE, and a whole panoramic view of a seashore and every little thing in it glides into view, like I am focusing a camera lens. It is much more spacious and expansive than the previous kind of vision but it is also much harder to maintain. It's more remote, lighter. It's like when you are dreaming and catch yourself noticing that you are dreaming, and as a result the whole thing vanishes to vapor. In contrast to the previous kind of imagining, I must stay more receptive to maintain the vision. I can "make" things in it, but almost everything else does not feel made by me. Staying connected is often tricky and unstable.

What's going on here? Do I have two different sets of "visioning" eyes? Should I be concerned that this faculty seems to be divided in me rather than unified?

Thanks so much!
Bonnie

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Excellent. You're beginning to perceive the two functions of imagination. The first is the ability to act on the astral plane; the second is the ability to perceive on the astral plane. Give them both time and attention, and you'll develop both powers -- they're both necessary to magical practice.

comments.title: How to explain magic to a loved one
comments.content:
Good afternoon. I'm a long-time reader and commenter, lately posting anonymously. I will try to tell my story briefly and then keep my question succinct.

I was raised in a fairly liberal Christian home, and in my early twenties had a series of difficult visionary and non-human entity encounters- difficult in part because the Christianity I was raised in had little to no interest in metaphysics, and no resources to help me emotionally with these particular phenomena.

Over the nearly ten years since, your writings have been a big help to me in finding language and concepts for those experiences. Slowly I've been overcoming my initial fear of the non-visible and have recently gotten in touch with a mystery school which you recommended to me and and which I've spent some time researching and building confidence in. (They're currently closed to new students for restructuring, but I am looking forward to their re-opening).

Two months ago I began dating a woman with whom I'd like to build a long-term relationship. She was raised in a slightly less liberal Christian home (compared to me), left the church in her early twenties, and has recently begun to tentatively explore Christian faith again.

Last week I summoned up the courage to tell her about my long-ago spiritual experiences and the quest I've been on since then to understand what happened to me and why. I told her that magic and Qabalah seem to be the best fit I've found for me to learn and develop further. She listened carefully and has responded gently, but it's all so far outside her experience that she's expressed surprise and some fear about the idea of invisible entities and magic.

This was a risk I knew I'd have to take when I initiated the relationship; now I'm looking for resources or advice for how to introduce the world of magic and spirit to someone thinking about it seriously for the first time.

I've thought of sharing with her your Well of Galabes, Dion Fortune's 'Psychic Self-Defense', or Gareth Knight's 'Experience of the Inner Worlds'. (I'm especially grateful now for the preservation of the Well of Galabes, with the comment threads intact). Do you have any other resource suggestions, or advice for how to proceed? Encouragement or tips from other commenters is welcome as well.

Thank you again for all your work.

replies.title: Re: How to explain magic to a loved one
replies.content: The book I recommend to anybody who wants to see how magic and Christianity fit together is Gareth Knight's Experience of the Inner Worlds. Since he's a devout Anglican Christian and writes from that standpoint, his way of talking about magic is very well suited for Christians to understand. Another book to follow up with is Anthony Duncan's The Christ, Psychotherapy and Magic -- the late Father Duncan was an Anglican priest. Certainly encourage her to read the Well of Galabes if she likes, but Knight's book is the first go-to resource.

comments.title: Help with Affirmation Wording?
comments.content:
I'm wondering if you could help with the wording of an affirmation that I'd like to put into use.

I've always had a lot of trouble dealing with other people, individually, but even more so when group dynamics are involved.

In one-on-one interactions I tend to do better, but I have trouble making connections with people. In larger groups, maybe beyond 3-4, I tend to freeze up entirely, I'm not necessarily anxious to begin with, but I'm very uncomfortable, and once I've been silent & uncommunicative for awhile, the anxiety starts to build from there. Whatever methods I have for relating with people individually, they don't work well in larger groups.

It's causing a lot of problems, especially because I almost can't go to family gatherings anymore, I can't hide how upset I am, even though in my family these are pretty benign, pleasant gatherings. I feel ridiculous, but I also can't seem to stop these reactions. I've also dropped out of some interest groups that I tried to participate in, I just couldn't find a way to interact in any normal sort of way.

I was thinking of something like: "I am comfortable around other people, I speak my mind freely, and I connect with others in beneficial ways."

I would like to find an affirmation that would combine the idea of relating with others (and maybe groups of people) more easily with the idea of making connections with others which would be beneficial & enjoyable for them and for me. I feel like I've isolated myself unnecessarily over my life, and would like to make steps to change that.

Thanks as always for your work, and these forums you offer for readers to pose you questions!

~ Daniel

replies.title: Re: Help with Affirmation Wording?

You need to figure out where this reaction is coming from or it'll just keep on coming back. Once you know what combination of issues (most likely rooted in unresolved childhood experiences) are holding you back, you can craft an affirmation to address those issues directly, and go from there.

comments.title: Rough few months
comments.content:
So… I had a TSW moment. The story is no doubt not terribly interesting but suffice it to say that I saw, via divination, something that shouldn’t have been possible, at least from a materialistic point of view.

Problem is… afterwards I dropped everything. All ritual, divination, meditation. All of it. I feel like I touched a live wire and now I’m wary about getting back at it. Anyone who has done electrical work and gotten zinged knows the feeling. You approach the electric panel with trepidation.

Other things have also affected this as the real world has had its issues dragging down my mood and my will power. I wouldn’t describe this as “freaked out” though I did have a moment around the same time that left me a bit shook. I was driving past a venerable old oak and projected a grandfatherly benevolent sort of image on to it. It in return cast back an image of eldritch horror that oozed hatred. I’ve never had that happen before. I feel bad for the tree as it sits at an intersection of two busy roads located in between two college towns. I think the cumulative effect of these experiences has been that of me enjoying the view of the scenery only to become acutely aware that the scenery was enjoying the view of me. That’s probably enough metaphors for now.

Anyway, no real question specifically. I have gotten back to doing things but at a much reduced rate and I’m working on my will power to get back in the ring. Any words of advice will be appreciated.

Thanks for making a place to allow folks to ponder on things like this.

replies.title: Re: Rough few months
replies.content:
If you can arrange to get some massage, that would be helpful. Fear leaves patterns of tension in the body that can take a long time, or massage, to get rid of.

Other than that, though, you've faced what one of the classics of my insufficiently misspent youth called "the first enemy of a man of knowledge": fear. You and you alone can decide whether you will back away from the Path, or return to it and push on.

comments.title: Hestia
comments.content:
I recently, out of the blue, felt like Hestia wanted to, for lack of a better word, get in touch?

Aside from prayer, which I am pursuing, is there an obvious gesture, ritual, color of candle or what have you that a reasonably competent GD practitioner with no experience working with any other entities might do for Hestia? (I am a former chef, still love to cook, especially directly over fire, so when I meditated on it, it didn't seem so strange really, but I have no clue what to do).

replies.title: Re: Hestia
replies.content: The classic approach is to get a statue or image of Hestia, place it on a shelf or other elevated place (the top of a dresser, etc.) with a nice cloth under it, and burn a little frankincense to her each day while reciting one of the traditional hymns (you can find some of them here). By classic, I mean that people like Iamblichus and Proclus were teaching people to do this back when Rome still had an emperor! After you've offered incense and verse, sit in front of the image and listen inwardly; she'll speak when she's ready to.

replies.title: Re: Hestia
replies.content:
The traditional offering to Hestia is barley.

I have interpreted that as tossing some uncooked grains onto the altar, but afterward, I'm not sure what to do with them.

There are several groups practicing reconstructions of ancient Greek religion, in some cases under the title of Hellenismos. Generally they decline to do animal sacrifice and have various workarounds. Some of these groups have websites. You can find some advice and examples of ritual practices to do at home, and perhaps citation of sources. If you were doing a traditional rite in which several Olympians were invoked, you would start with Hestia and i think also end with Her.

comments.content:
Hi JMG!

As someone just starting to get into occultism, I've wanted to work on implementing a daily divination practice. The trouble I've been having is that I'm a very skeptical person by nature, and I have an enormous amount of difficulty actually trusting the results.

If I were to do a tarot spread, and then reshuffle the deck and ask the same questions, it would be extremely unlikely for me to then draw the same cards. In fact, I could ask the same question multiple times and get completely different answers, due to probability. Wouldn't this invalidate the legitimacy of the reading, or the oracle as a whole?

Similarily, if I were to use a binary yes/no oracle to determine (for example) whether my offerings were accepted, even if they were accepted 100% of the time, I still have a 50/50 chance of drawing "no," and I'd have no way of determining whether I was receiving a genuine message from the gods, or whether it was just random chance.

Any advice on how to overcome these concerns?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: If you approach divination that way you will fail. How well do you think it would work for you to establish a relationship with another person if, at every moment, you were doubting if you could trust anything the other person said? My advice, therefore, is to leave divination alone until you can treat it with some degree of respect.

comments.title: Magic Marriage?
comments.content:
Hello JMG!

So two of my friends have asked me to marry them, (as the officiator of the ceremony, not a member of the marriage.) Legal stuff not yet started but there's plenty of time.

I'm a sucker for symbolism and ceremony, so I'm very happy with the role, although I'm 21 and have no previous experience with anything like this, and in general I'm open for any advice (from you or the commentariate? commentariot?) for this particular event as well.

Both members of the couple are into Reiki, with one currently practicing, I say that just to establish that they're into the alternate spirituality world already, and open to the subtler areas of reality.

Originally they pitched it to me as having me dressed up as the grim reaper to marry them, with a theme of "till death do us part." Although they're open to changing the ceremony around, and happy to let me make plans and see what they like.

Since the ceremony is being held in late autumn, and with their preferences, it seems somewhat appropriate to have a marriage ceremony with some kind of death and rebirth symbolism.....

I'm assuming that unless I put some kind of serious magical intent to that, it's not really gonna be anything more than fun drama, but if they want me too, and it seems appropriate and checks out in divination and all that, I'd like to.

Any reason you think a marriage ceremony with death rebirth symbolism with somewhat charged intent could go awry? Would you recommend avoiding such a combo, or keeping it more on the "fun drama" side?

Any ritual or natural magic tips or suggestions for such an occasion?

And in general, pointers to keep it fun, safe, and meaningful?

Thank you for all your help thus far,

-Wind Man

Magickal Background in very basic golden dawn, working knowledge of LBRP, GRP, LBRH, RC. Have not made tools or a consecrated altar or anything, recently got the go ahead through (partner's) divination to try experimenting more and being creative with my own rituals. I also own your encyclopedia of natural magic, have a developing passion for gardening, and am eager to start learning to combine those, though I haven't yet.

replies.title: Re: Magic Marriage?
replies.content:
Oh dear gods, no.

I let a couple talk me into doing a marriage ceremony with magical components and a mythological theme many years ago, when I was young and dumb. The marriage was a disaster, and it ended explosively in a way that carried out the mythological theme to the letter. If you do a wedding with a Grim Reaper theme there's a very good chance that one or both of them will die young.

Marriage is much more magically powerful than most people realize. The traditional symbolism is there for very good reason and I strongly advise you to advise them to stick with it, and at most throw in some funny costumes.

it seems I have some research to begin doing, and some exploration to due to the fact that both of them are pretty emotionally damaged by that particular christian tradition. More questions for next MM!

-WM

replies.title: Re: Magic Marriage?
replies.content:
In a nutshell, your responsibilities are:

1. To officiate a marriage ceremony that is legal in the place they are getting married. That includes making sure that they obtain a marriage license ahead of time and the proper form to fill out after the ceremony. Then you need to make sure to fill it out properly, get the two witnesses to sign it, sign it yourself, and mail it or hand deliver it to the proper office.

2. Unless the wedding is not going to have any guests, the wedding ceremony needs to be designed in a way that allows the guests to be happy for the couple, and it also needs to be recognizable to the guests as being a wedding ceremony. Since you have never attended a wedding ceremony, you are flying blind (other than any depictions of weddings in the movies or on TV, which usually are based on the Anglican ceremony in The Book of Common Prayer).

It sounds as if the couple is focused on their own experience to the exclusion of the feelings of the guests. You can balance that. Do a little research online or get a book or two on how to do a modern marriage, and get a clue about what the guests are going to expect and how they hope to participate. You don't have to cater to all of the guests' expectations, but it would be rude not to put them at ease and make them welcome.

Having guests has several purposes. The couple get to share their joy with others. The guests get to wish the couple well. Everyone gets to enjoy a celebration. The rite of passage from two single people to one married couple is recognized by the community. If any of those things are omitted without a good reason such as necessity, the marriage starts off on on the wrong foot.

People who are active in a religious tradition rather than alienated from one often get some preparatory counseling from their minister or rabbi. This of course assumes a prior relationship with a minister or rabbi and with the religion he or she practices.

Last year I helped to compose a marriage ceremony loosely based on a standard Jewish wedding ceremony. I went to an excellent Judaica store in Berkeley called Afikomen. The store had many different books on how to plan a Jewish wedding. I bought two that the owner recommended, one written from an Orthodox viewpoint and one more liberal. Both books had advice on things that the couple could do during the weeks ahead of the wedding to prepare themselves for the step they are going to take. This is a good idea, but if people don't know about it, they will just be caught up in all the mundane preparations rather than doing any inner work. You are not qualified to be their spiritual advisor, nor did you sign up for that, but if you come across something in a book that looks like it might be helpful, you could share it with them.

If you can, try to find an advisory book about weddings that is mostly secular in its outlook, but which focuses at least a little bit on people's love and happiness, rather than entirely on hiring the caterer and planning the floral arrangements. Then offer the couple some gentle suggestions. They aren't going to do anything they don't want to do, but you might be able to suggest ideas that hadn't occurred to them.

Good evening JMG!

A few questions about divination today.

You have mentioned that you should not make decisions on the result of a single divination but I also understand that asking the same question several times is a slippery slope. What I have come to realize is that one should think carefully about what is really being asked to the oracle, since it is not The Great CosmicOom to answer instantly, unquestionably and omnisciently. However, I still feel stuck into how tackle a question from different angles. Could you give me some advice into how to use an Oracle more wisely? I think I am in that threshold between the daily reading and using it more widely.

Related to the first one. How can one avoid falling into an echo chamber while using an oracle? I have fallen into one in the past, not related to divination but more of in a behavioral way and it is nasty. Would definitely want to avoid that in divination!

With zooming eagerness,
Heliotrope Tangential Eel

P.D I’ve discovered something in my thought patterns with our past Q&A interactions. Somehow, the way you reply in some instances leaves me puzzled, frustrated and then gloriously reflexive. I take it is not completely unintentional. Very grateful for inducing that in me! Or as a song I like says: “Oh the pain it is but the joy it brings!”

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) A great deal depends on what kind of oracle you're using. With a relatively flexible one like the Tarot or Coelbren, think about the question until you can construct a spread where each card gives you the information you need about some part of the issue. With a more fixed oracle such as geomancy or horary astrology, think about the issue until you can frame a set of questions, each of them different, each giving you some of the information you need.

2) Treat the oracle as a friend giving advice, not a source of infallible knowledge.

(No, it's not unintentional. Just one of the services I offer.)
